segments.purgatorio-9-g51: Tu se' is Dante's older spelling where se' is a contracted form of modern sei ('you are'); here it functions as the auxiliary verb with the past participle giunto to form a perfect sense ('you have arrived' / 'you are arrived'). Omai is an adverb 'by now/now', and al purgatorio is the prepositional phrase indicating destination/location; word order (subject pronoun — auxiliary — adverb — location — participle) is emphatic and typical of poetry.
segments.purgatorio-9-g52: Vedi is an imperative or hortative present ('see/look') addressed to tu. Là il balzo is the direct object ('there, the cliff/rocky ledge'); che introduces a relative clause ('that/which'). The sequence che 'l chiude dintorno contains an enclitic clitic 'l' (apostrophized lo) as a direct object, so the clause literally reads 'which encloses it around' (chiude = present indicative 'encloses'), with dintorno as an adverb 'around'.
segments.purgatorio-9-g53: Again vedi = 'look/see'. L'entrata is 'the entrance' (la entrata elided to l'entrata). Là 've is a fused locution ('là dove' → 'there where'), with ve = 'where'. Par is the archaic third-person singular of parere ('it seems'); digiunto functions as a past participle used adjectivally meaning 'remote/separate', so the clause means 'see the entrance there where it seems remote/removed.'
segments.purgatorio-9-g54: Dianzi is an adverb meaning 'earlier' or 'before' (poetic/archaic). Placed alone with a following comma it sets a temporal frame for the whole sentence.
segments.purgatorio-9-g55: Ne l’alba is an older form of 'nella alba' = 'in the dawn' (ne = in). Che introduces a relative clause modifying alba ('which/that'), and procede is a present indicative used poetically to mean 'advances' or 'moves on toward'; al giorno = 'toward the day'.
segments.purgatorio-9-g56: Quando begins a temporal subordinate clause 'when'. L’anima tua is the noun phrase 'your soul', dentro = 'within/inside', and dormia is the imperfect tense ('was sleeping' / 'was asleep'), describing an ongoing past state.
segments.purgatorio-9-g57: Sovra li fiori = 'over the flowers'. Ond’ is a contracted relative/adverb ('of/from which' or 'with which') referring forward to 'that place' being adorned; è là giù addorno literally 'is there below adorned'. Venne (passato remoto) is the narrative past 'came', and viene una donna is 'a woman came'.
segments.purgatorio-9-g58: E = 'and'; disse is passato remoto 'she said' (narrative past). This introduces direct speech.
segments.purgatorio-9-g59: I’ is the dialectal/poetic form of 'io' (I). Son is the present of essere meaning 'I am' (used in direct speech). Lucia is the proper name (Saint Lucy).
segments.purgatorio-9-g60: Lasciatemi is the imperative 'let me' (lasciate + mi) with the enclitic pronoun mi; pigliar is the infinitive 'to take' (pigliar = pigliare in prosa), so 'let me take'. Costui = 'this one/this man' is the direct object, and che dorme is a relative clause 'who sleeps' with dorme in the present indicative.
segments.purgatorio-9-g61: Sì here means 'thus' or 'so'; l’ is the clitic object 'him/it' (written as l' before a vowel) attached before the verb; agevolerò is first-person singular future 'I will ease/help' or 'I will assist him'; per la sua via = 'on/by his way' indicates manner ('by/along his way').
segments.purgatorio-9-g62: Subject + verb + coordinated noun phrase: 'Sordel' is the proper name subject, 'rimase' is passato remoto (simple past) meaning 'remained/was left'. After the conjunction 'e' the phrase 'l’altre genti forme' is a compressed noun phrase ('the other people/forms') with the article + adjective + noun order typical in Italian.
segments.purgatorio-9-g63: 'ella' is the third-person subject pronoun, 'ti' is the clitic direct object 'you' placed before the finite verb, and 'tolse' is passato remoto of 'togliere' meaning 'took (away)'. The clause therefore states a completed action: 'she took you.'
segments.purgatorio-9-g64: 'e' = 'and'; 'come' here functions temporally as 'when/ as' introducing the time clause; '’l dì' = 'the day' and 'fu' is passato remoto of 'essere' ('was'); 'chiaro' is the adjective 'clear/bright'. Together: 'and when the day was clear.'
segments.purgatorio-9-g65: This clause contains the particle/contracted form 'se n'' (written here as 'sen') meaning 'from there/away', followed by 'venne' (passato remoto of 'venire' = 'came') and the adverb 'suso' = 'upwards'. The structure indicates movement away/upwards: 'she came up from there.'
segments.purgatorio-9-g66: 'e io' = 'and I'; 'per' + accusative here means 'by/in the tracks of' (instrumental/route), 'le sue orme' = 'her footprints/tracks'. The short clause says the speaker followed by her traces.
segments.purgatorio-9-g67: 'Qui' is a locative adverb 'here'; 'ti' is the clitic direct object preceding the verb; 'posò' is passato remoto of 'porre/posare' meaning 'placed/laid (you)'. The clause says 'She placed you here.'
segments.purgatorio-9-g68: 'ma' = 'but'; 'pria' = 'before/earlier'; 'mi' is indirect object 'to me'; 'dimostraro' is passato remoto plural 'they showed/revealed'. Note the inverted order: the verb precedes its subject ('li occhi suoi belli' = 'her beautiful eyes'), and the object is the clause 'quella intrata aperta' (that open entrance), where 'intrata' functions as a nominal/participial form modified by the adjective 'aperta'.
segments.purgatorio-9-g69: 'poi' = 'then'; the clause lists two things that left together: 'ella' (she) and '’l sonno' (sleep, personified). 'ad una' (literally 'to one') is an idiom meaning 'together/at the same time', and 'se n’andaro' (se n' andarono) is passato remoto plural 'they went away from there' with the contracted particle 'n’' = 'ne' ('of/from it/there'). The phrase thus means 'then she and Sleep went away together.'
segments.purgatorio-9-g156: This whole phrase is a main clause with a following relative clause. 'Tale imagine' is the noun phrase subject ('such an image'); 'a punto' is an adverbial phrase meaning 'at once' or 'at that moment'; 'mi rendea' is an archaic imperfect (‑ea ending) of rendere with the clitic 'mi' (indirect object 'to me'), so 'rendered/made clear to me'. The relative clause 'ciò ch’ io udiva' ('that which I was hearing') uses 'ciò che' contracted to 'ch’' and 'udiva' (imperfect of udire) to report an ongoing past perception and links as the direct object of 'rendea'.
segments.purgatorio-9-g157: 'Qual prender si suole' is an impersonal/fixed expression: 'qual' ('as'/'such as') + infinitive 'prender' and the impersonal 'si suole' ('is wont/one usually'), so literally 'as one is accustomed to take'. The subordinate temporal clause 'quando a cantar con organi si stea' uses the preposition 'a' with the infinitive 'cantar' (old infinitival construction 'a cantar' = 'in singing' or 'to sing'), 'con organi' = 'with organs' (musical accompaniment), and 'si stea' is an archaic/poetic form of impersonal 'stare' (compare modern 'si stia' or 'si sta') meaning 'one stands/is positioned (to sing)'.
segments.purgatorio-9-g158: This is a result/clarifying clause introduced by 'ch’' (= 'che' = 'that'). The adverbial pair 'or sì or no' means 'now yes, now no' (i.e. sometimes yes, sometimes no). 'S’intendon' combines the clitic 'si' (impersonal/passive) with 'intendono' (present third plural of intendere) and here means 'are understood/heard' rather than 'intend'; 'le parole' is the feminine plural subject 'the words'.
segments.purgatorio-9-g128: A single-word vocative/exclamatory address: 'Cenere' (ash/ashes) stands alone and is set off by a comma as if the speaker is calling or identifying the thing being spoken about. Grammatically it is a nominative noun used in direct address (or as an appositive name for what follows).
segments.purgatorio-9-g129: 'o' here is an interjection/vocative particle ('O' / 'Oh'), followed by the noun 'terra' (earth). The relative clause 'che secca si cavi' qualifies 'terra': 'che' = 'that/which'; 'secca' is an adjective 'dry' modifying 'terra'; 'si cavi' is reflexive/impersonal (si) + 'cavi' (3rd person present subjunctive or poetic present meaning 'is dug out'/'is taken out'), describing what happens to the earth when dry. Word order places the exclamation and its qualifying clause together for emphasis.
segments.purgatorio-9-g130: A prepositional phrase beginning with the contraction 'd’' = 'di' ('of'/'from') + 'un' ('a'). 'color' (color/hue) is the head noun. 'fora' here is an archaic/poetic form (not the sense listed) — it functions adverbially (akin to 'fuora'/'fuori') meaning 'outwardly' or 'on the outside'. 'col' = 'con il' ('with the'), 'suo' is the possessive 'its', and 'vestimento' is 'garment/covering'. The phrase characterizes appearance: 'of an outward color with its garment.'
segments.purgatorio-9-g131: 'e' = 'and' links this clause to the previous. 'di sotto' is a locative phrase meaning 'from beneath' ('di' used with 'sotto' to indicate origin/location). 'da quel' = 'from that' (da + demonstrative). 'trasse' is passato remoto (third person singular) of 'trarre' meaning 'he drew/pulled out'. The object 'due chiavi' ('two keys') completes the action. Note the poetic placement of the locative phrase before the verb.
segments.purgatorio-9-g132: Simple descriptive clause: 'L’una' (short for 'La una' = 'the one') and 'l’altra' ('the other') are subjects. 'era' is the imperfect indicative ('was') used for description. 'd’oro' and 'd’argento' are adjectival prepositional phrases 'of gold' and 'of silver'. Word order is straightforward: subject — copula — predicate.
segments.purgatorio-9-g133: 'Pria' = 'primo/prima' ('first' / 'before') introduces an ordered sequence, followed by 'con la bianca e poscia con la gialla' ('with the white and afterwards with the yellow') — two instrumental prepositional phrases indicating how he used the keys. 'Fece' is passato remoto of 'fare' ('he made/did/put'); here it governs an implicit object (the act of using the keys) and is followed by 'a la porta' ('at/to the door') indicating the target. 'Sì' ('thus/so') modifies the manner or result. The clause compresses action: first one key with the white [something], then the other with the yellow, he used/applied them to the door.
segments.purgatorio-9-g134: Result clause begun by 'ch’' (= 'che', here 'that/so that/as a result'), followed by the enclitic form 'i’' = 'io' (old orthography for 'I'). 'Fu’' is the passato remoto ('was') and 'contento' is a predicate adjective ('content/pleased'). The whole means 'so that I was pleased' or 'and I was pleased,' with the contraction 'ch’' marking the result introduced by the preceding 'sì'.
segments.purgatorio-9-g135: Quandunque is a subordinating conjunction meaning "whenever". The noun phrase l’una d’este chiavi = "one of these keys" uses the elided article l’ before una; d’+este = di queste. Falla here is a noun/description meaning a fault or failure in the key (i.e. "is faulty" in sense), so the whole clause means "whenever one of these keys has a fault".
segments.purgatorio-9-g136: Che introduces the subordinate clause explaining the fault. Non si volga is negative + reflexive construction: volgersi = "to turn/rotate"; si marks the key as turning itself (middle voice). Volga appears in an older/poetic present (here conveying the general meaning "turns"). Dritta = "straight" modifies the (feminine) key, and per la toppa = "through the toppa/slot/hasp" (per = "through"). The clause means "that it does not turn straight through the slot/lock."
segments.purgatorio-9-g137: Diss' is a contracted poetic form of disse (passato remoto of dire), "he said." Elli is an archaic/poetic form referring to the speaker (he); a noi = "to us." The sequence is simply "he said to us."
segments.purgatorio-9-g138: Non s’apre is a negative reflexive/passive-like construction: aprirsi = "to open (oneself)" so non s'apre = "does not open." Questa calla means "this calla/way/gate" (calla here = a passage/gate). So the quoted speech finishes with "this gate/path does not open."
segments.purgatorio-9-g139: Più cara è l’una is a short inverted clause: Più = "more/rarer", cara = predicate adjective "dear/valuable," è = copular verb, and l’una = "the one." Word order places the comparative first for emphasis: "One is dearer."
segments.purgatorio-9-g140: Ma = "but." L’altra = "the other (one)." Vuol is the present of volere used to mean "requires/needs" here. Troppa modifies the following abstract complement d’arte e d’ingegno = "(too) much art and ingenuity/skill." Avanti che introduces a subordinate clause meaning "before" and governs a subjunctive (diserri) expressing what must happen first; the whole means "but the other requires too much skill and ingenuity before it can be unfastened."
segments.purgatorio-9-g141: Perch’ (short for perché) = "because." Ella = "it/she" (referring back to the 'other' key). È quella che introduces a defining relative clause: "is the one that..." ’l nodo = "the knot" (elided article ’l = il). Digroppa is a rare/poetic verb (digroppare) in the third person singular meaning to undo/loosen or otherwise release the knot; the clause says "because it is the one that loosens/undoes the knot."
segments.purgatorio-9-g142: Da = 'from' or 'by' (here marking origin/authority). Pier is Saint Peter (the proper name). le is the feminine plural clitic pronoun 'them' (written here as an article form in the lexical list); tegno is first person singular present of tenere 'to hold/keep'. Together the clause means 'I hold them from/by Peter' (i.e. I keep them by Peter's authority or as Peter's possession).
segments.purgatorio-9-g143: e dissemi is an archaic contraction of 'e disse a me' ('and he said to me'); ch' = che 'that'. i (here representing 'io') plus erri is the old inflection of errare meaning 'I err / I was wrong' as reported speech — Dante often preserves older personal forms. anzi introduces a contrast/preference 'rather/better'; ad + infinitive (ad aprir) is the infinitive marker 'to open'; ch' a (che a) introduces the alternative clause 'than to...' and tenerla is the infinitive tenere with the feminine clitic -la attached ('to keep it'), serrata is a past participle/adjective 'shut/closed' agreeing with the feminine object.
segments.purgatorio-9-g144: pur che here functions idiomatically as 'provided that' (an archaic concessive/conditional conjunction). la gente 'the people' is the subject; a' piedi 'at the feet' is a locative phrase (a + piedi); mi is a dative clitic 'to me' (they fall to me); s'atterri is the reflexive form of atterre (here meaning 'to bring down / to fall down / prostrate oneself') in the verbal form demanded by 'pur che' (a subordinate clause). The whole means 'provided that the people fall down/prostrate themselves at my feet.'
segments.purgatorio-9-g145: Poi is an adverb ('then'). pinse is 3rd person singular passato remoto of an older verb (here best understood as 'pushed' or 'forced'); it is the main past action. l’uscio is the direct object with the elided article (l' = il) + uscio 'door'. a la porta sacrata is a prepositional phrase ('to/at the sacred gate'); a + la is written separately (poetic/formal spacing instead of modern 'alla'), and sacrata is an adjective agreeing with porta (feminine).
segments.purgatorio-9-g146: dicendo is the present gerund of dire, used here to link the main action to the words that follow: 'saying' or 'and he said'. It indicates simultaneous or accompanying utterance rather than a separate tense.
segments.purgatorio-9-g147: Intrate is the 2nd person plural imperative (command) meaning 'enter' addressed to the group being spoken to; it begins the direct quotation.
segments.purgatorio-9-g148: ma is the adversative 'but'. facciovi is an archaic/reflexive verbal form (compare modern fatevi/fate) meaning 'make yourselves' or 'take care'—here 'be warned' or 'take heed'; it contains clitic particles (ci + vo/vi) fused in an older form. accorti is a predicate adjective (pl.) meaning 'aware/heedful'. che introduces the subordinate clause 'that ...'. di fuor (di + fuor) means 'from/out of the outside' (i.e. 'back outside'). torna is 3rd person singular present indicative or present historic sense 'returns/sends back' (here 'is sent back / turns back'). chi 'he who/ whoever' introduces the relative/indefinite subject. ’n dietro si guata is a reflexive phrasing: 'looks back' (si = reflexive, guata = look/peer back); the negative consequence in the main clause applies to anyone who does that.
segments.purgatorio-9-g149: E quando begins a temporal clause ('And when'). fuor + ne' (fuor ne’) means 'out of / from' (a poetic inversion: 'out of the hinges'). cardini distorti is a noun + adjective ('hinges twisted'); distorti agrees in number and gender. li spigoli is the definite article + noun 'the projections/points/edges' and di quella regge sacra is a genitive phrase 'of that sacred regge' (regge is an older/poetic word for a defensive structure/support/fortress here). Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect: the adverbial phrase and participle precede the subject.
segments.purgatorio-9-g150: che introduces a relative clause modifying 'spigoli'. di metallo is an ablative/partitive-like phrase 'of/with metal' specifying composition. son is the archaic contracted present plural of essere ('sono' → 'son') meaning 'are'. sonanti is an adjective/participle 'sounding, resounding', and forti 'strong' both agree with the plural subject.
segments.purgatorio-9-g151: non ... né combines two negated predicates: non rugghiò 'did not roar' (rugghiò = passato remoto 'roared') and né si mostrò 'nor did she show herself' (si mostrò is reflexive passato remoto). sì after the verbs is an intensifier 'so/that much'. acra is an adjective 'bitter/harsh' modifying the implied subject (Tarpëa), whose name follows in apposition.
segments.purgatorio-9-g152: come introduces the comparison 'as' or 'like'. tolto le fu is a periphrastic/passive-like sequence: tolto (past participle 'taken away') + fu (3rd sg. passato remoto of essere) yields 'were taken from her' or 'had been taken from her'; le is the feminine plural object pronoun referring back to 'spigoli' or the valuables. il buono Metello is an epithet + name ('the good Metellus') serving as the agent in the comparison.
segments.purgatorio-9-g153: per che is a causal/conclusive phrase 'wherefore / because of which', poi 'then/afterwards' is temporal, rimase is passato remoto 'remained', and macra is an adjective 'lean/gaunt' (feminine) describing the resultant state.
segments.purgatorio-9-g154: Io mi rivolsi: 'rivolsi' is passato remoto (1st sing.) of 'rivolgere'/a poetic variant 'rivolsare' and takes the reflexive clitic 'mi' (I turned myself). 'attento' is an adjective used adverbially/predicatively to indicate manner ('attentively' / 'my attention turned'), and 'al primo tuono' is a prepositional phrase ('to the first tone/peal') with 'al' = a + il.
segments.purgatorio-9-g155: The conjunction 'e' links the second clause. The Latin phrase 'Te Deum laudamus' is quoted as a unit (Te = 'thee/you', Deum = 'God' in the accusative of the hymn, laudamus = 'we praise'). 'mi parea' is an impersonal-like construction with the dative clitic 'mi' plus the imperfect 'parea' (it seemed to me). The infinitive 'udire' is the complement of 'parea' ('to hear'), and the final phrase 'in voce mista al dolce suono' describes the manner—'in a voice mixed with the sweet sound' (mista agrees with voce; 'al' = a + il).
segments.purgatorio-9-g104: Ed is the conjunction 'and' linking this clause to what went before. Ella is the feminine pronoun 'she' (here referring to the Blessed Lady) and is the subject; i passi vostri is the direct object (article + noun + postposed possessive: 'your steps'). In bene is a prepositional phrase meaning 'into good' or 'toward goodness', and avanzi here is a verbal form meaning 'advance/put forward' (an optative/subjunctive sense: 'may she advance'); the verb form is archaic/poetic.
segments.purgatorio-9-g105: Ricominciò is the passato remoto, 3rd person singular of ricominciare, 'he/it resumed' (here 'he resumed' or 'he repeated'). The subject follows the verb in the typical narrative word order: il cortese portinaio 'the courteous porter' (noun with adjective). The colon shows he is about to speak again.
segments.purgatorio-9-g106: Venite is the 2nd-person plural imperative (an invitation: 'come' or 'come here, all of you'). Dunque is an adverb 'then/therefore'. A (written here as a’) is the preposition 'to' (elided before a vowel); nostri gradi = 'our steps/steps of ours' (gradi = steps/stairs). Innanzi is an adverb meaning 'in front/ahead' or 'before us', specifying the direction.
segments.purgatorio-9-g107: Là is the locative adverb 'there'. Ne is a clitic pronoun here used adverbially with venimmo; the phrase dà a sense of place/motion in older usage (lit. 'thence' / 'therefrom') and venimmo is passato remoto, 1st-person plural 'we came'.
segments.purgatorio-9-g108: The clause strings modifiers before the verb: 'e' = 'and'; lo scaglion primaio = 'the first (large) step/landing' (lo + noun + adjective primaio 'first'). Bianco marmo is an appositive identifying the material ('white marble'), and era is imperfect 'was'. Sì pulito e terso is an intensifying predicate: 'so clean and bright/polished.'
segments.purgatorio-9-g109: Ch' is the contracted che 'that'. Io mi specchiai is passato remoto 1st sing. of specchiarsi, 'I saw myself reflected' (mi = reflexive pronoun). In esso 'in it' names the mirror-like surface, and qual io paio is a comparative/limiting phrase 'as I appear' (qual = 'as/how', paio here refers to his appearance).
segments.purgatorio-9-g110: Era is the imperfect 'was'. Il secondo 'the second' names the step; tinto is a past participle/adjective 'dyed/stained'. Più che is a comparative formula 'more than', and perso here has the archaic meaning of a very dark/tarry hue (so: 'darker than dark').
segments.purgatorio-9-g111: D’ is the contracted preposition di 'of'. Una petrina is 'a small paving-stone/slab' (petrina = small stone/flagstone). Ruvida 'rough' and arsiccia (from arsicciare) 'scorched, parched' describe its texture and color.
segments.purgatorio-9-g112: Crepata is a past participle used adjectivally, 'cracked/split'. Per lo lungo e per traverso is an idiomatic pair of prepositional phrases meaning 'lengthwise and crosswise' (lo lungo = along the length; traverso = across).
segments.purgatorio-9-g113: Lo terzo is simply 'the third' (lo + ordinal terzo). The article + ordinal is fronted for emphasis/naming the step.
segments.purgatorio-9-g114: Che is the relative 'which/that'. Di sopra is a locative phrase 'above' or 'on top', and s’ammassiccia is the reflexive present indicative 3rd-person singular of ammassicciare (s’ + ammassiccia): 'is heaped up' / 'piles up there'.
segments.purgatorio-9-g115: Porfido is the material-name 'porphyry' (a purplish igneous rock) used predicatively; mi is the dative/clitic 'to me', and parea is the imperfect 3rd-singular of parere 'seemed'. Together: 'seemed to me porphyry.'
segments.purgatorio-9-g116: Sì modifies the adjective fiammeggiante 'blazing/fiery' ('so blazing'). Come introduces a simile 'like', sangue 'blood' is the image, che is the relative 'that', fuor di vena = 'out of a vein' (fuor = out, di = of/from, vena = vein), and spiccia (from spicciare) means 'rushes/gushes out' — the whole simile: 'so blazing as blood that gushes from a vein.'
segments.purgatorio-9-g117: Sovra (preposition) precedes its object questo ('this') and means 'upon' or 'above'. Tenëa is an archaic poetic form of tenere (imperfect/indicative) meaning 'had/kept/placed' and is the main verb whose subject is l'angel; ambo le piante ('both the soles') follows the verb and names what he 'had' on the thing (i.e. he had both feet on it). The participial/gerund phrase sedendo in su la soglia ('sitting upon the threshold') gives the attendant circumstance (how he was positioned), while the relative clause che mi sembiava pietra di diamante follows and modifies soglia; mi is the dative 'to me' used with sembiava ('seemed to me'), an imperfect indicative describing Dante's perception.
segments.purgatorio-9-g118: Per li tre gradi sù is a prepositional phrase 'up the three steps' (Per + plural article li + gradi + sù 'up'). Di buona voglia is an ablative/qualifying phrase meaning 'with good will' or 'willingly'. Mi trasse is a past tense (passato remoto) of trarre ('he drew me' / 'he pulled me'), where mi is the object. Il duca mio is the subject in apposition 'my duke' (duca here = guide). Word order is typical for Dante: circumstantial phrases front the clause for emphasis.
segments.purgatorio-9-g119: Dicendo is the present gerund of dire ('saying') used to introduce direct reported speech that follows; it links the action of the duke to the words he utters.
segments.purgatorio-9-g120: Chiedi is an imperative ('ask!') addressing Dante; umilemente is an adverb 'humbly' modifying the imperative. The subordinate clause introduced by che contains a present subjunctive (scioglia) expressing an indirect command or purpose ('that he/it loosen'); l' serrame refers to the fastening or lock (serrame) and is the direct object of scioglia. In older/poetic syntax che + subjunctive is used after imperatives to introduce what is to be requested.
segments.purgatorio-9-g121: Divoto is an adjective used adverbially 'devout(ly)' describing the manner of the action; mi gittai is passato remoto of gittare ('I threw myself/cast myself'), reflexive with mi, and a’ santi piedi ('at the saints' feet') is the prepositional complement indicating where he cast himself. The syntax expresses a sudden, devotional movement towards the saints.
segments.purgatorio-9-g122: Misericordia chiesi is the main clause in passato remoto: 'I asked for mercy' (chiesi = I asked). The conjunction e links the next clause ch'el m'aprisse: ch' is the contracted che ('that'), el here stands for the thing to be opened (the article/pronoun 'it'), m' is the dative 'to me', and aprisse is the imperfect subjunctive of aprire used in reported/desiderative clauses after 'I asked'—'that it might open to me.'
segments.purgatorio-9-g123: Ma introduces the contrast 'but'. Tre volte ('three times') is the adverbial phrase of frequency; nel petto ('on my breast/chest') is the locative complement where he struck himself; prima ('before/first') orders the action in time; mi diedi is a reflexive/periphrastic idiom 'I gave myself' used to mean 'I struck myself' (i.e. struck his breast). Diedi is passato remoto of dare.
segments.purgatorio-9-g124: Sette P ne la fronte mi descrisse: Sette is the numeral 'seven'; P denotes the letter P (here the character traced on the brow). Ne + la fronte is the locative 'in/on the brow/forehead'; mi is the indirect object 'on me' and descrisse is passato remoto of descrivere used here to mean 'he traced/inscribed' (Dante often uses descrivere = to mark/trace). Col punton de la spada is an ablative instrumental phrase 'with the point of the sword' (punton = point; de la spada = 'of the sword').
segments.purgatorio-9-g125: E is the simple conjunction 'and'. Fa is an imperative form of fare here meaning 'see to it' or 'make (it so)'. Che introduces the subordinate clause where lavi is present subjunctive or imperative-like form meaning 'you wash' / 'you should wash'—Dante uses che + present subjunctive in commands.
segments.purgatorio-9-g126: Quando is the conjunction 'when'. Se’ is the contracted form of sei (you are) used in older/poetic Italian; dentro is an adverb 'inside'. Together they mean 'when you are inside' (i.e. once you have entered).
segments.purgatorio-9-g127: Queste piaghe is the direct object 'these wounds' (queste = these, piaghe = wounds/afflictions). Disse is passato remoto of dire ('he said') closing the reported speech; the entire line reproduces the angel's injunction.
segments.purgatorio-9-g92: "Dite" is the 2nd-person plural imperative of dire, used as a command 'say/tell'. "costinci" is a locative/adverbial form (archaic/poetic) meaning 'from/at that place' and follows the verb; the colon introduces the reported question that follows.
segments.purgatorio-9-g93: "che" is an interrogative pronoun here meaning 'what'; "volete" is present indicative 2nd-person plural of volere ('you want'); "voi" is the emphatic/resumptive subject pronoun 'you' (plural) appearing after the verb, a common word order in spoken/question forms.
segments.purgatorio-9-g94: A comma serving as punctuation to continue the discourse and separate the reporting clause from the preceding speech.
segments.purgatorio-9-g95: "cominciò" is the passato remoto (3rd singular) of cominciare, 'he began'; "elli" is an archaic subject pronoun meaning 'he' (here the subject of cominciò); "a" introduces the infinitive complement; "dire" is the infinitive 'to say' — together 'he began to say'.
segments.purgatorio-9-g96: "ov'" (elided form of ove/ov'è) is an interrogative adverb 'where'; the apostrophe marks elision; "è" is present 3rd-person singular of essere 'is'; "la scorta" is the noun phrase 'the escort' (la + scorta). The whole is a direct question: 'Where is the escort?'
segments.purgatorio-9-g97: "Guardate" is the 2nd-person plural imperative (or hortative) of guardare, here 'take care/watch out'. "che" introduces a subordinate clause of caution ('that' / 'lest'). "'l" is the elided definite article (lo) attached to the following infinitive used as a noun; "venir sù" (venire + sù) is an infinitive phrase 'coming up' used nominally; "non" negates the verb; "vi" is the clitic for 'to you' (indirect object); "nòi" is an archaic/dialectal form (final vowel dropped) of a verb meaning 'to harm / to trouble' (compare modern noia/nuocere) and functions as 3rd-person singular — overall: 'Be careful that the coming up do(es) not harm you.'
segments.purgatorio-9-g98: "Donna del ciel" is a vocative phrase: "Donna" (address 'Lady') plus "del" = di + il ('of the') and "ciel" is the poetic/archaic form of cielo meaning 'heaven' — an honorific address ('Lady of Heaven').
segments.purgatorio-9-g99: "di queste cose accorta" is a participial/ adjectival phrase modifying the vocative: "di" governs the following object "queste cose" ('these things'), and "accorta" (feminine singular) is a past-participle/adjective agreeing with "Donna" meaning 'aware' or 'mindful'.
segments.purgatorio-9-g100: "rispuose" (archaic spelling of rispose) is passato remoto 3rd-person singular of rispondere, 'answered'; "'l mio maestro" uses the definite article + possessive before the noun ('my master'); "a lui" is the indirect object 'to him' — together: 'my master answered him.'
segments.purgatorio-9-g101: "pur dianzi" is an adverbial phrase meaning 'even/just now' ("pur" as an intensifying particle + "dianzi" 'a little while ago'); "ne" is a clitic referring back to 'of it/about it'; "disse" is passato remoto 3rd-person singular of dire ('said') — 'she even a moment ago said of it...'.
segments.purgatorio-9-g102: "Andate" is the 2nd-person plural imperative of andare ('go'), here giving the command 'go (you all)'; "là" is the locative adverb 'there'. This begins the inner quoted instruction.
segments.purgatorio-9-g103: "quivi" is an archaic demonstrative/adverbial 'there'; "è" is present 3rd-person singular of essere 'is'; "la porta" is 'the gate/door' (la + porta). This completes the quoted direction: 'there is the gate.'
segments.purgatorio-9-g29: Ivi parea che = an impersonal expression 'it seemed there that...' where 'parea' is the imperfect tense of parere used impersonally. 'ella e io' is the compound subject ('she and I'), and 'ardesse' is the imperfect subjunctive of ardere used after 'parea che' to express a concurrent or hypothetical action — here best translated 'were burning'. Note the poetical word order and the subjunctive mood introduced by 'che'.
segments.purgatorio-9-g30: e sì lo ’ncendio imaginato cosse = 'and so the imagined fire spread'; 'sì' intensifies ('so'), 'lo' is the definite article attached to 'ncendio' (archaic contraction), 'imaginato' is a past participle used adjectivally ('imagined'). 'cosse' is a past (passato remoto) verb here meaning the fire 'ran' or 'spread' quickly — a vivid, kinetic verb applied to the imagined flame.
segments.purgatorio-9-g31: che convenne che ’l sonno si rompesse = 'so that/with the result that it was necessary that sleep be broken'; 'convenne' is an impersonal past ('it was necessary / it had to be') and introduces a subordinate clause with 'che' plus the imperfect subjunctive 'rompesse'. The clitic 'si' makes 'rompersi' reflexive/impersonal here — 'sleep was interrupted' or 'sleep broke'.
segments.purgatorio-9-g32: Non altrimenti = 'not otherwise/ no differently,' used to set up a simile. 'Achille' is the subject and 'si riscosse' is a reflexive verb in the passato remoto (si + riscosse) meaning 'he roused himself' or 'started up'; the reflexive 'si' marks that the action falls back on the subject.
segments.purgatorio-9-g33: li occhi svegliati rivolgendo in giro = 'his eyes, awakened, turning about'; 'li occhi' is the direct object ('the eyes'), 'svegliati' is a past participle used adjectivally ('awakened'), and 'rivolgendo' is a gerund ('turning'), with 'in giro' indicating movement 'around' or 'about'.
segments.purgatorio-9-g34: e non sappiendo là dove si fosse = 'and not knowing there where he was'; 'non sappiendo' is a present gerund/participle meaning 'not knowing', and 'si fosse' is the imperfect subjunctive of essere used in the subordinate clause ('where he might be' / 'where he was'), reflecting uncertainty or indirect description.
segments.purgatorio-9-g35: quando la madre da Chirón a Schiro trafuggò lui dormendo in le sue braccia = 'when his mother stole him away from Chiron to Scyros, carrying him asleep into her arms'; 'quando' introduces a temporal clause, 'trafuggò' is passato remoto meaning 'stole away,' 'lui' is the direct object, 'dormendo' is a gerund indicating the action occurred while he slept, and 'in le sue braccia' is a prepositional phrase 'into her arms.'
segments.purgatorio-9-g36: là onde poi li Greci il dipartiro = 'thence/whence later the Greeks separated him'; 'là' 'onde' form a relative phrase 'from there/whence', 'poi' is 'then/later', 'li Greci' is the subject, and 'dipartiro' is passato remoto meaning 'they parted' or 'took him away/separated him.'
segments.purgatorio-9-g37: che mi scoss’ io = 'which shook me' (with inversion for emphasis); 'scoss'' is a contracted form (apostrophe marks an omitted vowel) of a past-tense verb meaning 'shook/disturbed', with the clitic 'mi' (me) as the object and the subject 'io' placed after the verb for emphasis.
segments.purgatorio-9-g38: sì come da la faccia mi fuggì ’l sonno = 'just as sleep fled from my face'; 'sì come' introduces the simile, 'da la faccia' is a prepositional phrase 'from the face' (i.e. from my countenance/presence), 'mi' is an indirect object clitic, and 'fuggì' is passato remoto of fuggire — an intransitive past meaning 'fled/left.'
segments.purgatorio-9-g39: e diventa’ ismorto = 'and I became lifeless/pale'; 'diventa’' is a contracted past-tense form of diventare ('I became'), and 'ismorto' is an archaic/poetical adjective (written with an initial 'i' before 'smorto') describing a state like 'dead/pale/unconscious' — the apostrophe shows an omitted letter and the phrase is elliptical and condensed for metrical reasons.
segments.purgatorio-9-g40: come fa l’uom che = 'as a man does who'; 'come fa' is a present-tense general statement 'as (it) happens / as a man does', with 'l’uom' the generic subject and 'che' introducing the following relative clause.
segments.purgatorio-9-g41: spaventato, agghiaccia. = 'frightened, he freezes.' 'spaventato' is a past participle used adjectivally ('frightened'), and 'agghiaccia' is a present-tense verb meaning 'he stiffens/freezes' (literally 'becomes chilled/rigid'); together they complete the simile introduced by 'come fa l’uom che'.
segments.purgatorio-9-g42: Dallato m’era solo il mio conforto = 'Only my comfort was with me beside me'; 'Dallato' is an adverbial/locative 'at that side / beside', 'm'era' is the clitic 'mi' + imperfect 'era' ('was to me' / 'remained to me'), and the nominative 'il mio conforto' is the grammatical subject placed after the verb (inverted word order typical of poetry).
segments.purgatorio-9-g43: e ’l sole er’ alto già più che due ore = 'and the sun had already been high for more than two hours'; 'er'' is an archaic contraction of 'era' (imperfect 'was'), 'alto' is a predicate adjective 'high', and 'già più che due ore' literally 'already more than two hours' — 'più che' here functions as 'more than.'
segments.purgatorio-9-g44: e ’l viso m’era a la marina torto = 'and my face was turned toward the sea'; 'm’era' again is clitic + imperfect 'was to me', 'a la marina' is the prepositional phrase 'toward the seashore/sea', and 'torto' is used adjectivally to mean 'turned/turned aside' (poetic usage rather than the more literal 'crooked'), indicating the direction of his face.
segments.purgatorio-9-g45: A negative command formed with non + infinitive, a common older/poetic way to give an imperative: non + aver = 'do not have.' The subject 'you' is implied (second person). "tema" is the direct object (fear).
segments.purgatorio-9-g46: "disse" is the passato remoto (simple past) 'he said' used here as the reporting verb; it precedes the subject phrase (verb–subject order) for poetic rhythm. "il mio segnore" is a normal noun phrase with the possessive before the noun; "segnore" is an archaic spelling of signore (lord).
segments.purgatorio-9-g47: "fatti" is an imperative with an enclitic reflexive sense ('make yourself' / 'become'); here it functions as a second-person command meaning 'make yourself sure' or 'become certain/confident.' "sicur" is an archaic, metrically shortened form of sicuro ('certain/confident').
segments.purgatorio-9-g48: "ché" is an explanatory/causal conjunction ('for' / 'because') introducing the reason. "noi semo" is the present indicative 'we are' in a Tuscan/archaic form (semo = siamo); the explicit subject "noi" adds emphasis. "a buon punto" is an idiomatic prepositional phrase meaning 'at a good point/stage,' with "a" = 'at' and "punto" = 'moment/point.'
segments.purgatorio-9-g49: Another negative command. "non" negates the following verb form. "stringer" is the infinitive/stem form (the final -e is dropped for metrical reasons), used here as an imperative equivalent meaning 'do not tighten/strain/tense (yourself).' The form is archaic/poetic but the sense is a second-person prohibition.
segments.purgatorio-9-g50: "ma" = 'but' introduces the corrective command. "rallarga" is an affirmative imperative (second-person singular) meaning 'expand/loosen again' or 'restore/renew,' telling the addressee to broaden or recover strength. "ogne" is the archaic form of "ogni" and, as usual in Italian, takes a singular noun: "vigore" = 'strength/vigor.'
segments.purgatorio-9-g70: A guisa d’uom = a fixed simile phrase meaning "in the manner of a man." The preposition a + guisa (= manner) introduces the comparison; d’uom is the contracted di uomo (of a man). The two relative clauses introduced by che describe that man: si raccerta is reflexive (rassicurarsi / rassicurare se stesso) meaning "reassures himself" and muta ... sua paura inverts subject and possessive (the fear is the subject that "changes" into comfort). Verbs are present indicative here to express a general or immediately perceived fact within the simile.
segments.purgatorio-9-g71: poi che = "after/when" (introduces the temporal clause). La verità li è discoperta uses li as a dative pronoun "to him" (the truth is revealed to him); discoperta is a past participle used predicatively with essere (is revealed/discovered). Word order places the pronoun before the auxiliary, typical in older Italian.
segments.purgatorio-9-g72: mi cambia’ io = archaic past form: cambia' (with apostrophe) stands for cambiai (passato remoto) and is first person; the clitic mi precedes the verb as a reflexive/object pronoun and io after the verb is emphatic/explanatory. Literally "I changed (myself)" — here "I changed" or "I was transformed."
segments.purgatorio-9-g73: e come sanza cura vide me ’l duca mio = "and as, without care, my guide saw me." sanza cura (= senza cura) is an adverbial phrase meaning "carelessly/without anxiety." Vide is passato remoto (he saw); the object clitic me stands before the subject noun phrase '’l duca mio' because Italian allows clitics before full NP, and the postverbal NP is emphatic/identifying the subject.
segments.purgatorio-9-g74: su per lo balzo si mosse = "he moved up along the ledge." su per = "up along/over;" lo balzo is the physical feature (a rocky ledge or sudden rise). Si mosse is riflessive/passive-form muoversi in passato remoto (si + mosse) meaning "he moved"; subject (the guide) is omitted because it was just named.
segments.purgatorio-9-g75: e io di rietro inver’ l’altura = "and I behind, toward the height." di rietro = "from/behind" (a phrase meaning "in back"), inver' is an archaic contraction of verso ("toward"), and l’altura = "the height/hillside." Word order places io for emphasis: "and I (went) behind toward the height."
segments.purgatorio-9-g76: Lettor is a vocative address to the reader (archaic spelling of lettore). It directly addresses the audience before the explanation that follows.
segments.purgatorio-9-g77: tu vedi ben com’ io innalzo la mia matera = "you see well/how I raise my matter." Vedi is present indicative (you see/you perceive), ben intensifies it. com’ is a contraction of come (how/as). Innalzo is present indicative "I raise/exalt," and matera (materia/materia poetica) means "subject matter" or theme — the word order places the object phrase after the verb.
segments.purgatorio-9-g78: e però con più arte non ti maravigliar s’io la rincalzo = "and therefore, with more skill, do not be surprised if I reinforce/expand it." Non ti maravigliar is a prohibitive (don't be amazed) where the infinitive maravigliar stands for an imperative (older usage). con più arte = "with greater skill/artifice." s’io = contraction of se io ("if I"); rincalzo here means to add to or reinforce the subject; word order places the conditional clause after the prohibition.
segments.purgatorio-9-g79: Noi ci appressammo = passato remoto first-person-plural appressammo (we approached). The clitic ci indicates the action was toward/for ourselves or simply marks reflexive/reciprocal approach; subject Noi is explicit.
segments.purgatorio-9-g80: ed eravamo in parte che là dove pareami prima rotto = "and we were in the place that there, where it had seemed to me before, (was) broken." Eravamo is imperfect (we were); in parte means "in the place/part"; the following che introduces a relative/location clause. pareami is a poetic/archaic compound (parea + mi) meaning "it appeared to me" in the imperfect; prima rotto = previously broken/breached.
segments.purgatorio-9-g81: pur come un fesso che muro diparte = a simile: pur come = "just as"; un fesso here is a fissure/cleft (nominative) rather than "a fool." che introduces the relative clause; muro diparte uses diparte (present) meaning "parts the wall" — poetic present used for vividness.
segments.purgatorio-9-g82: vidi una porta = vidi (passato remoto) = "I saw"; una porta = "a door/gate." Simple narrative past: narrator perceptually notices the door.
segments.purgatorio-9-g83: e tre gradi di sotto per gire ad essa = "and three steps below to go toward it." tre gradi = "three steps/grades," di sotto = "below" (specifying position). per + infinitive gire = purpose/intent "in order to go" (per gire ad essa = to reach it). The infinitive after per expresses purpose.
segments.purgatorio-9-g84: di color diversi = "of different colours." The noun color (colors) takes the adjective diversi after it; the whole phrase modifies the steps/door just mentioned. This is normal adjective agreement (plural masculine diversi).
segments.purgatorio-9-g85: e un portier ch’ancor non facea motto = "and a porter who as yet made no sound/word." Portier (portiere) = doorkeeper/porter; ch’ancor non facea motto uses ancor (ancora) "still/yet" and facea (imperfect of fare) "did/made" plus motto = "utterance/word"; idiomatically "non facea motto" = "made no move/no sound/was silent."
segments.purgatorio-9-g86: E come l’occhio più e più v’apersi = "And as the eye opened more and more there." più e più = "more and more" (iterative intensifier). v’apersi is a contracted reflexive construction (vi + aprirsi) where vi indicates location/there and apersi is poetic past of aprirsi (opened). The clause sets up the moment of perception.
segments.purgatorio-9-g87: vidil seder sovra ’l grado sovrano = "I saw him sit upon the sovereign step." vidil is the combination vidi + l' ( vidi 'l = I saw him/it ); this is followed by an infinitive (seder) in the typical perception construction 'vidi + object + infinitive' meaning 'I saw him sit.' sovra = above/on; grado sovrano = highest/supreme step.
segments.purgatorio-9-g88: tal ne la faccia ch’io non lo soffersi = "such in his face that I could not endure it." tal = such/so great; ne la faccia = in his face/appearance (ne = in/of it, here locative/partitive use), and the result clause introduced by ch' (che) gives the narrator's inability to bear the sight; soffersi is perfect/past sense "I endured/suffered" used negatively.
segments.purgatorio-9-g89: e una spada nuda avëa in mano = "and he had an unsheathed/bare sword in his hand." avëa (avea) is the imperfect of avere (had) with trema marking vowel; nuda = bare/unsheathed; in mano = in (his) hand. The clause describes the weapon held by the figure.
segments.purgatorio-9-g90: che reflettëa i raggi sì ver’ noi = "which reflected the rays so toward us." che introduces a relative clause modifying the sword; reflettëa (reflettea) is imperfect "was reflecting/used to reflect." i raggi = the rays; sì = "so" (intensifier) and ver' is an archaic truncation of verso meaning "toward" (ver’ noi = toward us). This phrase explains the brightness that affected the narrator.
segments.purgatorio-9-g91: ch’io drizzava spesso il viso in vano = "so that I often turned/raised my face in vain." ch' = che (that/so that) introduces the consequence; drizzava is the imperfect of drizzare meaning "to raise/turn/direct" (here 'turn one's face toward it'); spesso = often; in vano = in vain. The imperfect indicates repeated, unsuccessful attempts.
segments.purgatorio-9-g1: La concubina di Titone antico is a straightforward noun phrase: definite article + noun + genitive modifier 'di Titone antico' (of ancient Tithonus). già s’imbiancava is an imperfect-reflexive verb (s’ = si, reflexive) meaning 'was becoming white' or 'was growing white' (imperfect progressive sense). al balco d’orïente places the action spatially: al = a + il 'at/on the' + balco 'balustrade/balcony', d’orïente = 'of the East' (poetic spelling).
segments.purgatorio-9-g2: fuor de le braccia del suo dolce amico: 'fuor' (archaic variant of fuori) = 'outside' or 'out of'; 'de le braccia' = 'of the arms' (here 'braccia' = embraces). 'del suo dolce amico' = 'of her sweet friend/lover' (possessive suo agrees with amico). Word order is normal for a prepositional phrase describing location.
segments.purgatorio-9-g3: di gemme la sua fronte era lucente inverts the more neutral 'la sua fronte era lucente di gemme' for poetic effect: di gemme ('with gems') modifies fronte; era lucente is the imperfect of essere + adjective ('was shining'). This is descriptive, not a completed action.
segments.purgatorio-9-g4: poste in figura del freddo animale: poste is the past participle of porre ('placed' or 'set') agreeing with the feminine plural gemme (understood); in figura = 'in the shape/figure', del freddo animale = 'of the cold animal' (an animal associated with cold — the image described). che con la coda percuote la gente is a relative clause: che = which/that; percuote is present indicative 'strikes'.
segments.purgatorio-9-g5: e la notte: simple coordination 'and the night'; article + noun. The clause that follows attributes actions to 'la notte' (night) as a subject.
segments.purgatorio-9-g6: de’ passi con che sale: de’ (di + i) passi = 'the steps', 'con che sale' = 'by which it rises' (relative use where 'che' refers back to passi or to notte). This is a poetic way to speak of the night by its ascending steps toward dawn.
segments.purgatorio-9-g7: fatti avea due nel loco ov’ eravamo: unusual/poetic word order (past participle + auxiliary + numeral). fatti (past participle of fare = 'made/done') + avea (imperfect of avere) = 'had made' or 'had formed'; due = 'two' (steps). nel loco ov’ eravamo = 'in the place where we were' (ov' = ove, 'where').
segments.purgatorio-9-g8: e ’l terzo già chinava in giuso l’ale: 'and the third was already inclining/bowing its wings downward.' chinava is the imperfect of chinare ('to bend/tilt'), in giuso = 'downward' (poetic), l'ale = 'the wings' (ale, poetic plural of ala). The imperfect describes a continuing posture.
segments.purgatorio-9-g9: quand’ io, : 'when I' — quand' is the elided form of quando before a vowel; introduces the speaker's action that follows.
segments.purgatorio-9-g10: che meco avea di quel d’Adamo: a relative clause modifying io ('who had with me...'). meco = 'with me' (enclitic adverb), avea = imperfect 'had', di quel d'Adamo = 'of that one of Adam' (a terse way to identify a companion 'that one, of Adam' — genitive construction). The clause identifies someone the narrator had with him.
segments.purgatorio-9-g11: vinto dal sonno: vinto is the past participle of vincere, 'overcome' (used predicatively with dal sonno = 'by sleep'); this phrase functions as a participial clause explaining the narrator's condition.
segments.purgatorio-9-g12: in su l’erba inchinai là ’ve già tutti e cinque sedavamo: in su l'erba = 'upon the grass'; inchinai is the first-person past-tense (passato remoto or simple past) 'I bowed/leaned down' (verb form irregularity in the provided lemma list), là 've (là 've = 'there where') + già tutti e cinque sedavamo = 'where already all five of us were sitting' (sedavamo = imperfect 'we were sitting').
segments.purgatorio-9-g13: Ne l’ora che comincia i tristi lai la rondinella presso a la mattina: 'Ne' (= in/at) introduces 'l'ora' (the hour). The relative clause 'che comincia i tristi lai la rondinella' has subject la rondinella (the little swallow) and verb comincia (present 'begins') with direct object i tristi lai ('the sad lays'). presso a la mattina = 'near morning' (= at dawn).
segments.purgatorio-9-g14: forse a memoria de’ suo’ primi guai: 'forse' = perhaps; 'a memoria di' = 'in memory of' or 'remembering'; suo' = archaic possessive 'her/its' (apostrophe marks contraction); primi guai = 'first troubles/sorrows' (guai = woes/troubles).
segments.purgatorio-9-g15: e che la mente nostra, : continuation 'and that our mind,' where 'che' introduces a new clause describing the speaker's mental state. 'la mente nostra' = 'our mind'.
segments.purgatorio-9-g16: peregrina più da la carne e men da’ pensier presa: peregrina ('wandering' or 'pilgrim') modifies mente; più da la carne e men da’ pensier presa is a comparative construction: 'more taken by the flesh and less by thought.' presa is a feminine past participle agreeing with mente: 'taken/occupied.'
segments.purgatorio-9-g17: a le sue visïon quasi è divina: 'a' here introduces the realm/relation 'in/to her visions'; le sue visïon = 'her visions' (visïon is poetic spelling of visione). quasi è divina = 'is almost divine' (quasi modifying the adjective divina). The whole phrase says the mind becomes almost divine in its visions.
segments.purgatorio-9-g18: in sogno mi parea veder sospesa un’aguglia nel ciel con penne d’oro: 'in sogno' = 'in a dream'; mi parea = 'it seemed to me' (impersonal construction with dative pronoun mi); veder (infinitive) complements parea; sospesa is the participle 'suspended' describing the eagle (un’aguglia) positioned nel ciel; con penne d’oro = 'with golden feathers.'
segments.purgatorio-9-g19: con l’ali aperte e a calare intesa;: 'with the wings open' (con l'ali aperte). 'e a calare intesa' is a participial/adjectival phrase: a calare = 'to descend' and intesa (feminine) = 'intent/aimed' agreeing with aguglia, so 'intent on descending.'
segments.purgatorio-9-g20: ed esser mi parea là dove fuoro abbandonati i suoi da Ganimede,: another impersonal construction 'ed esser mi parea' = 'and it seemed to me to be' (infinitive esser after parea). 'là dove fuoro abbandonati i suoi' uses the archaic fuoro (were) — past tense — with abbandonati (past participle) agreeing with i suoi ('his/their people'), 'da Ganimede' marks the agent 'by Ganymede.'
segments.purgatorio-9-g21: quando fu ratto al sommo consistoro.: 'quando' = when; fu ratto = 'was snatched/rapt' (ratto here = abduction/rapture, perfect tense with essere); al sommo consistoro = 'to the highest consistory' (sommo = highest, consistoro = consistory/court — mythic heaven).
segments.purgatorio-9-g22: Fra me pensava: 'Among myself I thought' is an idiomatic way to say 'I thought to myself.' fra me = 'within myself/among myself'; pensava is the imperfect 'I was thinking/I thought.'
segments.purgatorio-9-g23: 'Forse questa fiede pur qui per uso,': 'Forse' = perhaps; questa = 'this one/this thing'; fiede is a poetic verb form (here 'strikes' or 'does harm/acts') — the exact lexical root is archaic but context gives 'acts/strikes'; pur qui per uso = 'even here by custom' (pur = even/still; per uso = by custom/usage).
segments.purgatorio-9-g24: e forse d’altro loco disdegna di portarne suso in piede’.: 'and perhaps from another place it disdains to carry it up on foot.' disdegna is present 'disdains/refuses', di portarne = 'to carry (of it)' where -ne enclitic refers back to the object, suso in piede = 'upward on foot' (suso = up).
segments.purgatorio-9-g25: Poi mi parea che, : 'Then it seemed to me that' — poi = then; mi parea = 'it seemed to me' (impersonal construction), che introduces the subordinate clause describing what seemed.
segments.purgatorio-9-g26: poi rotata un poco, : 'after/then having turned a little' — rotata is a past participle (feminine) or perfect participle 'having turned', un poco = 'a little'. The participial phrase modifies the subject of the subordinate clause (the eagle).
segments.purgatorio-9-g27: terribil come folgor discendesse, : 'terrible as lightning it would descend.' folgor = 'lightning' (poetic); discendesse is the imperfect subjunctive (or imperfect in indirect speech) used after parea che to render the action that seemed about to happen — here translated as a past/future-in-the-past 'would descend.'
segments.purgatorio-9-g28: e me rapisse suso infino al foco. : 'and would carry/snatch me up as far as the fire.' rapisse is an imperfect-subjunctive form (rapire) used in the same indirect-style construction ('parea che ... rapisse') and means 'seize/carry off.' sus o/infino al foco = 'up to the fire' (infino = as far as).

sentences.purgatorio-9-s9: You have now reached Purgatory: look there at the cliff that encloses it all around; look at the entrance there where it seems remote.
sentences.purgatorio-9-s10: Earlier, in the dawn that advances toward day, when your soul was sleeping within, over the flowers with which that place below is adorned there came a woman, and she said: "I am Lucia; let me take this one who sleeps; so I will help him on his way."
sentences.purgatorio-9-s11: Sordel remained, and the other human shapes; she took you, and when the day was clear she went up from there; and I followed in her footprints.
sentences.purgatorio-9-s12: Here she put you down, but before that her beautiful eyes showed me that entrance open; then she and Sleep went away together.
sentences.purgatorio-9-s37: At once such an image made clear to me what I was hearing, as is customary when one stands to sing with organs; now the words are understood, now they are not.
sentences.purgatorio-9-s29: Ashes, O earth that, when dry, is dug up, of an outward color with its covering; and from beneath that he drew two keys.
sentences.purgatorio-9-s30: One was of gold and the other was of silver; first with the white and then with the yellow he applied [them] to the door thus, that I was made/content.
sentences.purgatorio-9-s31: "Whenever one of these keys is faulty — when it does not turn straight in the keyhole," he said to us, "this gate will not open."
sentences.purgatorio-9-s32: One is dearer; but the other requires too much skill and ingenuity before it can be unfastened, because it is the one that loosens the knot.
sentences.purgatorio-9-s33: I keep them from Peter; and he told me that I was wrong — better to open than to keep it shut, provided that the people fall down at my feet.
sentences.purgatorio-9-s34: Then he pushed the door toward the sacred gate, saying, "Enter; but be warned that whoever looks back will be sent back outside."
sentences.purgatorio-9-s35: And when the hinges were twisted outward and the projections of that sacred rampart, which are sonorous and strong with metal, did not roar so nor did Tarpëa show herself so bitter as when the good Metellus had them taken from her, wherefore afterwards she remained lean.
sentences.purgatorio-9-s36: I turned my attention to the first tone, and "Te Deum laudamus" seemed to me to be heard in a voice mingled with the sweet sound.
sentences.purgatorio-9-s21: “And may she put your steps forward into goodness,” resumed the courteous porter: “Come then onto our steps ahead.”
sentences.purgatorio-9-s22: We came there; and the first stair of white marble was so clean and polished, that I saw myself in it just as I am.
sentences.purgatorio-9-s23: The second was dyed darker than black, of a small rough and scorched stone, cracked lengthwise and crosswise.
sentences.purgatorio-9-s24: The third, which is heaped up above, seemed porphyry to me, so blazing as blood that gushes out from a vein.
sentences.purgatorio-9-s25: Upon this the angel of God rested both his feet, sitting on the threshold that seemed to me a stone of diamond.
sentences.purgatorio-9-s26: My duke drew me up the three steps willingly, saying, "Ask humbly that the fastening be loosened."
sentences.purgatorio-9-s27: Devoutly I cast myself at the saints' feet; I begged for mercy and that it open to me, but first I struck my breast three times.
sentences.purgatorio-9-s28: He traced seven P's on my brow with the point of his sword, and said, "Make sure you wash these wounds when you are inside."
sentences.purgatorio-9-s17: Say from there: what do you want?
sentences.purgatorio-9-s18: , he began to say, "Where is the escort?"
sentences.purgatorio-9-s19: Mind that coming up may not harm you.
sentences.purgatorio-9-s20: "Lady of heaven, mindful of these things," my master answered him, "even just now she said of it: 'Go there: there is the gate.'"
sentences.purgatorio-9-s5: There it seemed that she and I were burning; and the imagined fire spread so much that sleep had to be broken.
sentences.purgatorio-9-s6: No differently did Achilles rouse himself — his eyes awakened, turning about and not knowing where he was — when his mother stole him away from Chiron to Scyros, taking him while he slept into her arms, whence later the Greeks separated him; thus I was shaken, just as sleep fled from my face and I became senseless, like a man who, frightened, freezes.
sentences.purgatorio-9-s7: Only my comfort remained to me beside me, and the sun had already been high for more than two hours, and my face was turned toward the sea.
sentences.purgatorio-9-s8: "Have no fear," my lord said; "be sure of yourself, for we are at a good point; do not strain yourself, but renew all your strength."
sentences.purgatorio-9-s13: Like a man who, reassured of his doubt and whose fear turns into consolation when the truth is revealed to him, I changed; and when my guide, without concern, saw me, he moved up along the ledge, and I followed behind toward the height.
sentences.purgatorio-9-s14: Reader, you can plainly see how I exalt my subject, and therefore, with greater skill, do not be surprised if I enlarge it again.
sentences.purgatorio-9-s15: We drew near, and we were at the spot where, as it had seemed to me before, there was a breach; just as a cleft that parts a wall, I saw a door, and three steps below to go to it, of differing colors, and a porter who as yet made no sound.
sentences.purgatorio-9-s16: And as the eye opened more and more there, I saw him seated on the sovereign step; such was his countenance that I could not endure it; and he had a naked sword in his hand, which so reflected its rays toward us that I often turned my face in vain.
sentences.purgatorio-9-s1: The concubine of ancient Tithonus was already turning white at the eastern balustrade, outside the arms of her sweet lover; her brow shone with gems, set in the form of the cold animal that with its tail strikes people; and the night, by the steps by which it rises, had made two [steps] in the place where we were, and the third was already bowing its wings downward; when I, who had with me that one of Adam, overcome by sleep, bowed down on the grass where already all five of us were sitting.
sentences.purgatorio-9-s2: At the hour when the little swallow begins its sad lays, near the morning, perhaps in memory of her first sorrows, and when our wandering mind, taken more by the flesh and less by thoughts, is almost divine in its visions, in a dream it seemed to me to see an eagle suspended in the sky with golden feathers, with its wings spread and intent on descending; and it seemed to me to be where his had been abandoned by Ganymede, when he was snatched up to the highest consistory.
sentences.purgatorio-9-s3: I thought to myself: 'Perhaps this one strikes even here by custom, and perhaps from another place it refuses to carry it up on foot.'
sentences.purgatorio-9-s4: Then it seemed to me that, after turning a little, it would descend terrible as lightning, and would snatch me up aloft as far as the fire.
